prompt_md: What is the key difference between a `constexpr` function and a `consteval` function?

options.a: `consteval` functions run faster at runtime because they skip the function call overhead.
options.b: A `constexpr` function may be evaluated at runtime; a `consteval` function must always be evaluated at compile time.
options.c: `consteval` functions can call non-`constexpr` helper functions; `constexpr` functions cannot.
options.d: A `consteval` function can only return integer types.

explanation_md: `consteval` declares an immediate function: every call must be a compile-time constant expression. If it is not, the compiler emits an error. `constexpr` functions have a fallback to runtime when called with runtime arguments. Options a, c, and d are all incorrect.
`triple(4)` = 12. `triple(2)` = 6, then `triple(6)` = 18. Both are compile-time calls (arguments are constant expressions), so `consteval` is satisfied. The program prints `12 18`.

options.a: `consteval` functions are not allowed to return `int`.
options.b: `compute` is not `constexpr`, so calling it inside `consteval hash_it` is a compile error.
options.c: `hash_it(5)` must be assigned to a plain `int`, not `constexpr int`.
options.d: `consteval` functions cannot call any other function.

explanation_md: A `consteval` function body must only call `constexpr` (or `consteval`) functions, because its body must be evaluable at compile time. `compute` is a plain (non-`constexpr`) function, so calling it inside `hash_it` is a compile error. Fix: mark `compute` as `constexpr`.

options.a: Inside a `consteval` function to check whether the call is valid.
options.b: Inside a `constexpr` function to choose between a compile-time algorithm and a runtime implementation.
options.c: Inside any function to check whether the program is in debug mode.
options.d: Inside `main()` to detect whether optimization flags were passed to the compiler.

explanation_md: `std::is_constant_evaluated()` returns `true` when the enclosing expression is a constant expression (compile-time evaluation). It is most useful inside a `constexpr` function where you want different logic at compile time versus runtime — for example, a pure-arithmetic path at compile time and a library call at runtime.

The program compiles and prints `7`. `add_ce` is `consteval` but it is never called in `main`, so its existence causes no error. `add_cx` is `constexpr` and is called with `x` (a runtime `int`) — that is legal because `constexpr` functions can run at runtime. The result is `3 + 4 = 7`.
